sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 96, level 3.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 3. SPACE enables character movement (LEFT/RIGHT). Character at cols 9-20, P-shape at cols 45-56. UP/DOWN moves S bar. Goal unclear - possibly move character to overlap with P-shape or Y-shape.
- Plan: Try moving character RIGHT to see if it can overlap with P-shape or complete the level.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 15 of about 75 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After the RIGHT move, there's now only ONE B object with pixels > 30 (besides the bottom bar). The boundary shows:
[[21, 36], [21, 38], [29, 38], [30, 39], [30, 44], [29, 45], [27, 45], [27, 56], [29, 56], [29, 54], [30, 53], [32, 53], [32, 36]]
This looks like the character and P-shape have merged into a single object! The boundary combines parts of both shapes. The character was at cols 36-47 (after moving RIGHT by 3 from 33-44) and the P-shape was at cols 45-56. They now overlap at cols 45-47, and they've merged into one object.
But the level is not completed yet. So merging the character and P-shape is not the final goal. Maybe I need to do something else after merging them.
Looking at the image, the merged shape is now a larger black shape with white dots. The yellow shapes are still in place.
Maybe the goal is to move the merged shape to overlap with one of the yellow shapes? Or to move the S bar to a specific position?
Let me check if I can still move the character (now merged with P-shape). Let me try LEFT or RIGHT.
Actually, wait. The character and P-shape merged. Now I have a single black object. Maybe I need to move this merged object to overlap with a yellow shape to complete the level.
Looking at the yellow shapes:
- id=3: Y-shape at cols 33-44, rows 3-14 (top-right F-shape)
- id=4: Y-shape at cols 9-20, rows 9-14 (top-left L-shape)
- id=20: Y-shape at cols 9-20, rows 42-47 (bottom-left)
- id=21: Y-shape at cols 33-44, rows 42-53 (bottom-right)
The merged shape is at cols 36-56, rows 21-32. It's horizontally aligned with the top-right F-shape (cols 33-44) and bottom-right F-shape (cols 33-44).
Maybe I need to move the merged shape UP or DOWN to overlap with one of the yellow shapes? But earlier, UP/DOWN moved the S bar, not the character. Let me try UP/DOWN now that the shapes are merged.
Actually, let me first check if the merged shape can still move LEFT/RIGHT.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Level 3. Character and P-shape merged into one object at cols 36-56. Level not completed. Need to figure out next step - possibly move merged shape to overlap with yellow shapes.
Plan: Try moving merged shape UP/DOWN to see if it can overlap with yellow shapes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Current B objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] > 30:
        print(f"  pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try UP to see if merged shape moves
action(['UP'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter UP:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] > 30:
        print(f"  pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
print("level_completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
